Welcome to part four of Sales Mastery on Lease, the NoBS system for closing sales like crazy. In this segment, we're going to go into depth about a very important topic that would be? Closing. The sale. Closing the sale. We got the fashion plate here of GKIC and also the sales manager extraordinaire, Nick Leasey and Dan Kennedy. So let's get into closing. We talk a lot about that you're always closing. Like your entire presentation should be a close. What does that mean? So people will divide this into pieces. Right. And they will then get to an abrupt point that is like awkward and uncomfortable for everybody. Right. Right. And so really a sales presentation should be as assumptive as possible. And it should be have been set up to the greatest extent possible by marketing so that it can legitimately be assumptive. So like the ultimate of that is where somebody is coming knowing price or knowing price range, knowing they are coming to buy. Right. Right. So then it falls apart only if you mess something up. Right. Right. But the close should be at least seamless. Not okay. Now here's the call to action. You know there's environments where you sort of have to do that, a TV infomercial is one of them. Right. Right. And it is jarring. It's like, oh here it comes. Right. Yeah. And so there's structural elements to this. There's tone, the assumptive tone of here's how this is gonna work. Here's how you're gonna use this. Here's how we're gonna install it. Here's how, from the very beginning. The other point that you raise is almost more attitudinal than it is mechanical. That you don't view it as getting to a point where now there's gonna be a lot of difficulty and you have to crank up the power here and you have to drag or push somebody across an imaginary line. It should feel to you just as it feels to them like a natural progression from beginning to end. Yeah. And there's a whole energy aspect of it too. And you can see this and you can feel it. When somebody gets, when they think about their presentation, okay I'm doing this. Now we broke it apart into six steps, but that's just to break it apart. You don't actually wanna think about that and you can feel when an amateur salesperson is going into the close, right? They change their cadence of their voice. They change their whole energy level, their body tenses. It just doesn't work. Also I think people structure their presentations completely the wrong way. They start at the beginning as opposed to okay, here's what my outcome is going to be and now I gotta build everything to achieve that outcome. You know, I couldn't agree more. I think it's painful to watch when the person on the other end is uncomfortable with closing and they get flustered or they start talking faster, start talking slower, they get staccato and it's just, it is painful. And I think it begins with, they didn't begin with the end in mind. And I think they haven't come to the mental grips. And we talked a little bit about that in the last segment. The mental grips about, you know, if you truly believe that your product is the best solution for the prospect, then it's really just a natural byproduct of you asking to move forward with the relationship. Just like I firmly believe that everybody, every business owner should have magnetic marketing on their product. We totally believe that. Beyond a shadow of a doubt, you know, wake me up in the middle and I've gone to my head, I would say that. It's the important thing. So every conversation should have that level of certainty that I know this will change your business life just like it changes my business life. And I think it's just part of the process. And I think it comes with maturity. I think it comes with professionalism. I think it comes with taking the time. And I think it comes with taking the respect for the buyer to practice it and to become comfortable with it.
